U.S. Strategic Oil Releases Buy Time - But Not Much More

Since the start of the war with Iran and effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. petroleum inventories have fallen at some of the fastest rates on record, acting a shock absorber to avert fuel shortages and limit price rises.

The United States has become the supplier of last resort, accelerating exports of crude and refined products to the rest of the world, making up for some of the oil lost from the Persian Gulf.

The government owned Strategic Petroleum Reserve has functioned as intended to forestall panic buying and hoarding, minimise the impact on the national economy, and allow additional time for diplomatic and military planning.  

But the rate of inventory depletion is unsustainable, which explains the increasing urgency to find a way to re-open the Strait through negotiations or the provision of military protection to tankers transiting the waterway ...

Iran’s Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi:

The Strait of Hormuz is, without a doubt, under the sovereignty of Iran and Oman. There is no international waterway in the Strait of Hormuz.

For many years, this waterway was open to all ships. Iran and Oman ensured its security and provided services; until now, all services were free of charge.

However, the future management of the Strait of Hormuz will not be like the past. No one can challenge Iran and Oman's sovereignty over the strait.

The management of the Strait of Hormuz will not return to the pre-war system.

Goldman Slashes 2027 Brent Outlook to $80

Goldman Sachs cut its average 2027 Brent crude forecast to $80/bbl, from $85, pointing to robust non-OPEC supply growth and structural demand weakness, led by China’s EV push and efficiency gains.

It held Q4 2026 at $90/bbl, noting the Hormuz disruption’s price impact has been milder than expected thanks to softer demand.

Goldman is doubling down on the fundamentals over geopolitics thesis.

Even with ongoing Strait risks, they see supply flooding in and demand softening enough to cap longer-term prices.

Goldman Sachs just cut its 2027 Brent crude estimate to $80 per barrel.

The revision is based on two factors: stronger supply from non-OPEC producers and structurally weaker demand from China.

Goldman estimates that over 10% of China's demand decline becomes permanent as the country diversifies its energy mix away from oil.

This matters because China has been the marginal buyer driving global oil demand growth for two decades. A structural shift changes the long-term calculus for crude.

Brent recently dropped 6.2% in a single session. WTI fell 5.9%. These moves may reflect the market repricing not just short-term supply concerns, but longer-term demand uncertainty.

The contrast is notable: short-term volatility is getting headlines, but the bigger story is a major bank revising its multi-year outlook lower based on permanent demand destruction.

Worth watching whether other banks follow with similar revisions.

Short-term volatility, but the real story is structural demand - watch China.

$USO #Oil #GoldmanSachs #CrudeOil #Markets #FinTok #Investing

🛢️ Goldman Sachs Just Trimmed Its 2027 Brent Oil Outlook, What This Means for Energy Markets

In a notable shift, Goldman Sachs has lowered its forecast for the average 2027 Brent crude oil price to $80 per barrel, down from a previous estimate of $85.

This revision reflects a more cautious long-term view, driven primarily by expectations of robust global supply growth, particularly from non-OPEC producers, meeting softer-than-anticipated demand. Factors like moderating economic growth in key consuming regions, efficiency gains, and the ongoing energy transition appear to be weighing on the demand side, while ample inventories and renewed production capacity ease earlier tightness concerns.

Why This Matters:
For Producers & Energy Companies: A lower price trajectory could pressure margins, especially for higher-cost operators. It signals a return toward structural balance rather than prolonged scarcity, potentially capping upside for oil equities and related sectors into 2027.

For Investors: This aligns with broader market sentiment from institutions like the EIA, which also sees prices easing toward the high-$70s/low-$80s range as supply responses kick in. However, geopolitical risks (e.g., ongoing tensions in key chokepoints) remain wild cards that could spark short-term volatility.

Macro Implications: Cheaper oil supports consumer spending and helps tame inflation, but it may challenge oil-exporting economies and investment in new upstream projects.

Oil markets are notoriously cyclical, today’s bearish revisions can quickly flip with any supply disruption or demand surprise. Goldman’s move highlights how rapidly forecasts evolve amid shifting fundamentals.

What’s your take? 
Will we see $80 Brent hold as the new normal in 2027, or do you expect volatility to push prices higher on unexpected catalysts? Drop your thoughts below 👇

#Oil #BrentCrude #EnergyMarkets #GoldmanSachs #Commodities #Investing

Goldman Sachs $GS cut its 2027 average Brent oil forecast to $80 on weaker demand, but says oil $CL could spike to $140 if the Strait of Hormuz stays shut for about a year. 🛢⚠️

...anything that gets shipped, because $90 oil doesn't stay at the refinery. It moves through everything.

Goldman isn't panicking and isn't revising down. That's the signal. In a week where oil has been pulled in every direction by trade war fears and demand uncertainty, the bank looked at all of it and kept its number exactly where it was.

At $90 a barrel, petrol prices stay elevated. Not crisis-level, but well above the sub-$70 lows we saw earlier this year. The relief at the pump that drivers briefly felt doesn't fully come back.

The hidden pressure is on industries that run on diesel and freight. Supermarkets, logistics companies, manufacturers. Their input costs stay high. That feeds into prices on the shelf, quietly, over months.

Goldman's refusal to cut the forecast is essentially a bet that the world keeps consuming oil faster than it can be replaced by alternatives. That bet has been right for two years running.

text:
Market Summary

Equities
The S&P 500 finished Friday on a positive note, rising 0.5% to close at 7,431.46. Broader equity indexes showed resilience as the Dow gained 0.7% to top 51,202, steadying investor sentiment ahead of next week's inflation updates and ongoing corporate tech assessments.

Energy & Macros
WTI Crude Oil felt substantial downward pressure on Friday, sliding 3.2% to settle at 84.88 per barrel. The broader energy pull dragged gasoline down, though Natural Gas managed a slight gain to end at 3.120. Regional grain haulers and biofuel processors are monitoring how this energy volatility intersects with the final implementation details of the upcoming 45Z clean energy tax credits.

Metals
Gold futures staged a modest rebound on Friday to finish at 4,227.12 per ounce, recovering slightly from the sharp liquidations that drove prices to multi-month lows earlier in the week. Spot silver followed the base metals complex higher, closing out the week near 28.19 per ounce.

Grains & Livestock

Corn: July futures edged slightly higher to close at 4.13 per bushel. The market remains tethered to short-term retail infrastructure limits, balancing regional demand with expectations for high summer acreage.

Soybeans: July soybeans hovered near four-month lows, closing down at 11.13 per bushel. Prices face pressure from excellent Midwest growing conditions and a lull in immediate export demand, despite initial crop quality metrics sitting at 66% Good-to-Excellent.

Wheat: Chicago wheat futures ended the session down at 5.85 per bushel. While global feed competition remains steep, traders are weighing localized quality downgrades against tightening domestic winter wheat yields.

Livestock: Cash cattle premiums continue to hold above the board. August Live Cattle finished at 240.95 cents per pound, while August Feeder Cattle settled down at 357.05 cents per pound as high feedlot supplies and seasonal summer grilling volumes adjust pipeline flows.

Inside Look

Mud might not be the main driver right now, but excellent regional weather across the central Corn Belt has the technical charts locked in a tight baseline trap. Physical supply reality is outpacing the trading board this week. While the long-term push for year-round E15 provides solid baseline support for corn demand, processing facilities are watching tight shipping spreads closely. Over in the livestock barns, physical cash sales at regional auctions are still holding onto a premium over futures, showing that true packer demand for fat cattle is far more stubborn than Chicago's math implies.

#AgMarketing #Commodities #LivestockMarket

Weekly Futures Surprises for the week of June 8–12, 2026. This report tracks performance from the Monday open through the Friday close.

Top Futures Instrument: June Nasdaq 100 (/NQ)
The tech juggernaut remains completely unstoppable, logging its eighth consecutive weekly gain. Even a hot 6.5% year-over-year PPI print couldn't derail the momentum, as the highly anticipated SpaceX IPO successfully priced and unleashed a massive wave of fresh capital and retail enthusiasm back into growth assets.

Monday Open: 29,777.50
Friday Close: 30,124.75
Weekly Change: +347.25 (+1.17%)
Bias: Strongly Bullish
Trend: Record Extension
Outlook: The absolute floor has moved up to 29,800. Momentum models indicate institutional desks are completely looking past rate-hike whispers to chase structural enterprise software earnings.

Biggest Surprise Loser: June WTI Crude Oil (/CL)
Crude oil suffered another absolute drubbing this week, breaking wide open to lose more than 6% from its weekly opening prints. The unexpected driver was a dual blow of rising domestic inventory builds and a total collapse of the geopolitical risk premium as formal maritime safety corridors began stabilizing shipping flows.

Monday Open: $91.30
Friday Close: $84.85
Weekly Change: -$6.45 (-7.06%)
Bias: Strongly Bearish
Trend: Support Breakdown
Outlook: The war floor has officially turned into resistance. With macro desks unwinding supply-shock hedges, oil is highly exposed to a rapid retest of the $80.00 psychological level.

The Top Markets Surprise: SpaceX (SPACE) IPO Debut
The absolute story of the week across global finance was the massive, record-breaking market debut of SpaceX on Wall Street. The immense liquidity drain that choked alternative asset classes for the last two weeks finally culminated in a historic opening surge. The stock’s immediate post-listing velocity caught institutional short-sellers completely flat-footed, single-handedly lifting broader equity index sentiment on Friday afternoon.

The "6.5% PPI Shock"
Bond desks were hit with a massive volatility spike on Friday morning after the U.S. Producer Price Index landed at a scorching 6.5% year-over-year. While this heavily reinforced the hawkish faction of the Federal Reserve and pushed terminal yield expectations higher, equity indices completely swallowed the bad news within hours of the print.

Bitcoin's Defensive Base
Bitcoin futures (/BTC) managed a cautious green finish to end the week near $63,422. The surprise was the asset's underlying resilience; despite facing intense macroeconomic pressure from hot PPI data and an aggressive multi-billion dollar institutional rotation out of crypto ETFs and into the SpaceX IPO over the last fortnight, buyers aggressively defended the $60,000 baseline.

Weekly Scorecard:

Nasdaq 100 (/NQ): +1.17% (8th Straight Weekly Gain)
Bitcoin (/BTC): +2.66% (Defensive Support Bounce)
S&P 500 (/ES): +1.75% (SpaceX IPO Liftoff)
Gold (/GC): -1.21% (Hawkish Rate Pressure)
Crude Oil (/CL): -7.06% (Risk Premium Evaporation)

#FuturesMarket #SpaceXIPO #OilCollapse

Friday Market Closing Report: Friday, June 12, 2026

Wall Street advanced today as a fresh wave of diplomatic optimism in the Middle East and a truly historic corporate market debut gave investors the green light to reverse last week’s technical sell-off. The S&P 500 rose 0.5% and the Dow Jones Industrial Average added more than 350 points, clearing a path back into positive territory for the week. While sticky long-term Treasury yields kept a modest lid on peak valuations, a major retreat in global crude benchmarks provided much-needed breathing room for the broader, rate-sensitive economy.

Top Markets Story: SpaceX Surges 29% in Historic Wall Street Debut

The capital markets witnessed history today as Elon Musk's SpaceX (SpaceX) officially went public in the most highly anticipated Wall Street debut in years. Priced at an initial $135 per share, the stock exploded out of the gate to close its first trading session up 29.6% at $174.99. The first-day rally instantly vaulted the aerospace giant's market capitalization past the $2 trillion mark, injecting massive liquidity into the primary equity landscape and proving that institutional demand for high-growth, secular tech monopolies remains incredibly deep.

Futures and Commodities

The commodity pits faced an aggressive unwinding of "war premiums" today following an influx of concrete headlines regarding the shipping lanes in the Persian Gulf. Global energy contracts plunged into deep red territory, which in turn sparked a sharp short-covering reversal in beaten-down precious metals.

Top Futures Story: Peace Optimism Knocks Crude Down as Reopening Timelines Emerge

Energy futures suffered heavy liquidations on Friday following reports that ongoing diplomatic channels have established a firm framework to permanently defuse maritime friction. In a major structural update, global ratings agencies published base-case projections expecting the vital Strait of Hormuz to fully reopen to international merchant traffic within the coming weeks. The news prompted energy desks to aggressively price out the localized supply bottleneck, sending West Texas Intermediate (WTI) and Brent crude futures lower as traders prepare for the global market to return to an oversupplied state by late summer.

Market Mover Summary

SpaceX Mania: Shares skyrocketed nearly 30% above their IPO print, completely dominating trading volume and lifting the broader industrials and defense sectors.
Apple Intelligence Bid: AAPL edged higher to finish a robust week, as investors continued to digest the rollout of its next-generation "Siri AI" platform and upgraded foundation models showcased at its annual developers conference.
Semiconductor Rebound: Broadcom (AVGO) and chip equipment peers caught a strong institutional bid, clawing back a portion of last Friday's steep overvaluation flush.
Logistics Relief: National shippers and retailers advanced broadly as the sharp leg down in crude prices promised immediate relief for corporate transportation margins.

Highlights

Weekly Recovery: The S&P 500 locked in a 0.6% weekly advance, successfully erasing the psychological sting of its previous Friday rout.
The $2 Trillion Milestone: SpaceX immediately joined the elite tier of global mega-caps on its day-one public market performance.
Yield Relief: Benchmark Treasury yields fell nearly 10 basis points across the curve, with both the 10-year and 30-year yields breaking back below critical overhead thresholds.
Dollar Reversal: The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) surrendered its recent safe-haven gains, breaking back below the 100 level as global macro anxieties temporarily thawed.

Bonds, Commodities & Volatility

U.S. Dollar Index (DXY): 99.80 — Pulling back as capital rotates back into risk assets.

Gold Futures: $4,710.00 | D: +1.44%

Volatility (VIX): 14.90 (-5.70%)

Week in Review and Outlook
The "Liquidity Wave" Reality: This week proved that the structural depth of the 2026 equity market is fully capable of absorbing massive shifts in asset allocation. Between Apple's comprehensive AI architecture declarations and the multi-billion-dollar onboarding of SpaceX, institutional capital demonstrated that it has plenty of dry powder when the right secular growth narrative presents itself.
Looking Ahead – Key Events & Levels to Watch (June 15–19)

Formal Shipping Protocols: Traders will be monitoring maritime channels closely over the weekend for an official sign-off on the Gulf transit framework.
Warsh Policy Watch: Fixed-income markets are bracing for upcoming economic addresses to see how Chairman Warsh intends to balance the recent dip in energy costs against persistent service-sector wage growth.

SpaceX Volume Stabilization: Expect intense secondary market volatility early next week as the initial public offering momentum transitions into standard institutional positioning.

Closing Summary
Friday, June 12, was a clear victory for market optimists. While the macroeconomic debate surrounding interest rates will continue to create long-term noise, the day belonged to structural milestones. The combination of a historic corporate listing and tangible steps toward clearing global energy chokeholds provided the perfect antidote to last week's anxieties. The bulls are heading into the weekend back in command of the tape.

Technically, Brent crude dropped to $87.16 per barrel, while WTI fell to $84.67 after losing short-term bullish momentum. A sustained move below $88 for Brent could open the door toward the $85–86 zone, while a recovery above $88.50 may signal renewed buying interest.

Markets are now awaiting official confirmation of the agreement and its potential impact on global energy supplies, which could determine oil’s next major move.

💸 AryaFin Session Performance Audit | Friday, June 12, 2026

The Macro Overview: Wall Street extended its powerful geopolitical relief rally into Friday's close, logging modest single-day additions across all major domestic benchmarks. Market sentiment remained firmly backstopped by a secondary 3.4% slide in crude oil prices, fueled by growing institutional optimism regarding a potential U.S.-Iran peace deal.  

The undisputed centerpiece of the session, however, was the primary market arena, where SpaceX ($SPCX) executed its highly anticipated, record-breaking Wall Street public debut—surging nearly 20% on its first day of trading to confirm insatiable baseline institutional demand for elite aerospace and AI-linked networks.  

📊 Major Domestic Benchmarks Closing Matrix

* S&P 500 Index ($SP500): 🟢 +0.50% up 37.16 points to settle at 7,431.46.  
* Dow Jones Industrial Average ($DJI): 🟢 +0.70% up 353.51 points to settle at 51,202.26.  
* Nasdaq Composite ($COMP): 🟢 +0.30% up 79.18 points to settle at 25,888.84.  
* Russell 2000 Small-Cap Index ($RUT): 🟢 +0.80% up 22.96 points to settle at 2,943.99.  

⚡ Cross-Asset Velocity & Friday Drivers

The SpaceX ($SPCX) Capital Explosion: Launching at its fixed pricing baseline of $135.00 per share, SpaceX completely shattered modern underwriting records. Intraday order book pressure launched the stock 19.2% higher to finish its historical first trading day at $160.92, safely materializing its targeted multi-trillion dollar valuation baseline.  

Oil Capitulation Eases Inflation Anxiety: As top-tier diplomatic channels continued evaluating de-escalation framework guidelines, international oil benchmarks capitulated further. Brent crude futures slipped 3.4% to carve out multi-month relative lows around $87.25 per barrel, removing massive chunks of structural energy-input cost pressures from corporate balance sheets.  

AI Technology Rotational Mismatch: While primary demand centers locked onto the SpaceX float, the rest of the high-beta artificial intelligence and semiconductor cohort traded with massive mixed variance after staging an explosive 7.9% short-squeeze on Thursday.  

The Strategy View: Weekly Consolidation Completed. Friday's market action successfully solidified the structural turnaround carved out on Thursday afternoon. 

By matching a strong macro retreat in crude oil with a historic capital absorption event via the SpaceX public launch, equity markets demonstrated that deep institutional dip-buying appetites remain unbothered by sticky short-term CPI numbers. 

Keeping portfolios tilted toward cash-generative secular themes makes absolute technical sense as we head into next week's highly critical FOMC baseline policy meeting.  

#MacroAudit #WallStreet #SpaceXIPO #SPCX #OilPlunge #S&P500 #AryaFin

⭕️ Associated Press reported that the Islamic Republic's control over the Strait of Hormuz has decreased

The Associated Press wrote in an analytical report that the Islamic Republic's control over the Strait of Hormuz is decreasing.
According to this American news agency, at the same time, economic pressure on Tehran has increased sharply.
According to this report, oil exports from the Persian Gulf Arab countries through the Strait of Hormuz are increasing due to the support of the US military.
The news agency confirmed that more than 100 million barrels of oil have entered the world market through this route in recent weeks.
Donald Trump had announced similar statistics in previous days.
The Associated Press also wrote that the US naval blockade has faced serious problems for the Islamic Republic's oil exports, and Iranian oil production has decreased by about 800,000 barrels per day since the blockade began.
According to this report, the increase in oil reserves, the decrease in exports and the restriction of access to foreign currency have increased the pressure on the Islamic Republic to reach a diplomatic solution.

#Strait_of_Hormuz #Tawaf

President Trump has agreed to allow Iran to dilute its stockpile of highly enriched uranium within the country under the supervision of the IAEA, a U.S. official told Axios.

Other details include:

- A 60-day extension of the ceasefire, including in Lebanon.

- The reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

- The lifting of the U.S. blockade.

- Sanctions relief allowing Iran to sell oil internationally.

Breaking: Iran's foreign minister says a 14-point US–Iran memorandum of understanding is near completion — and serves notice that the Strait of Hormuz is no longer free by default.

Abbas Araghchi's announcement covers far more than a nuclear framework. On the Strait of Hormuz, he was unambiguous: the waterway sits under Iranian and Omani sovereignty, not international maritime law, and "the future administration of the Strait of Hormuz will not be like before." Roughly 20% of global oil supply transits that chokepoint daily. Iran is naming its leverage explicitly, and attaching it to the deal's fate.

Regional scope of the agreement:

The memorandum would declare an end to active conflict across all fronts, commit both parties to respect national sovereignty, and — Araghchi stated this explicitly — require Israeli withdrawal from occupied territory in southern Lebanon. Implementation of the MOU is a precondition for entering the second phase; if the provisions are not honoured, final agreement negotiations will not proceed.

On the nuclear file:

Sanctions relief and enrichment questions have been deferred to a 60-day second phase of negotiations. Iran's position on its stockpile of 60% enriched material is that any resolution must occur inside Iran through dilution — not export, not removal. The programme stays; the terms of its management are what remain to be negotiated.

Israel named as primary saboteur:

Araghchi identified the Israeli government as the deal's "foremost enemy," actively searching for pretexts to derail it. The public announcement itself functions as a diplomatic pre-emption: by placing the framework on the record in detail, Iran ensures that any collapse of the process will have a named, attributable cause.

It also includes the “suspension of sanctions on the sale” of Iran’s oil and petrochemical products and “the complete lifting of the US naval blockade” of Iranian ports, which has been in place since April 13.

Crude inventories in the Gulf are increasing as millions of barrels of crude oil a day bypass regulations in the Strait of Hormuz. Traders and ship trackers report the shift. $WTI

President Trump called for the capture of Kharg Island then a peace agreement hours later, whipsawing oil futures during Thursday’s trading session. WTI for August delivery, /CLQ26, settled at $86.16 per barrel, down 2.18 from previous settlement. 

Thursday’s WTI session opened with renewed threats against Iran, sending August futures as high as $91.62 per barrel. In a Truth Social post, President Trump said the U.S. would strike Iran that night and could take control of Kharg Island in the future. Hours later, he announced progress toward a Memorandum of Understanding, which pressured WTI to an intraday low of $84.30 per barrel.

Iran had not confirmed an agreement at the time of the announcement, with confirmation reportedly not coming until early Friday morning. The gap between headlines and confirmation has been a recurring feature of the conflict and may have contributed to reduced market confidence. The volatility also prompted the Senate Agriculture Committee to send a letter to the CFTC requesting a review of recent futures-market activity.

Kharg Island has remained a focal point for Trump given its importance to Iranian oil exports. Kharg is a small island in the Persian Gulf which processes ~90% of Iran’s oil exports, roughly 2.6 million bpd with capacity of 7 million bpd. The capture of this island would cripple Iran’s ability to get their oil to market, however, it would also prompt retaliatory strikes against US military personnel and other oil infrastructure in the region, further tightening global supplies.

A potential agreement may reduce some of the geopolitical risk premium, but supply normalization would likely take time. Inventories outside commercial storage would also need to be rebuilt. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve has declined by roughly 64 million barrels since the start of the year, to about 349 million barrels.

The latest EIA report showed a broad draw in petroleum stocks. Commercial crude inventories fell by 7.2 million barrels to 426.5 million barrels, while gasoline inventories rose by 0.2 million barrels to 215.1 million barrels. Distillate inventories declined by 0.2 million barrels to 102.1 million barrels. Refinery inputs averaged 17.0 million barrels per day, up 80,000 barrels per day from the prior week, with refineries operating at 95.3% of capacity.

Technicals
WTI August crude broke through the 50-day simple moving average of 88.14 but trading well above the 200-day SMA. 

The 14-day RSI settled at 44.63% and indicates a weakening in price as it heads towards the oversold threshold of 30%. 

The directional moving index indicates a strengthening bearish trend. The ADX is low but rising at 11.58. The positive directional index is low and weakening at 17.81 and below the negative directional index of 23.26, which is rising.

⚠️ 🇮🇷 Explosions Reported Near Sirik Tied to Hormuz Tanker Incident

Explosions heard near the southern Iranian city of Sirik on June 12 have been linked by local authorities to naval operations intercepting an oil tanker that tried to force its way through the Strait of Hormuz without following coordination procedures.

Iranian Revolutionary Guard sources explained that the blasts in the waters off Sirik resulted from efforts to stop and warn the non-compliant vessel during a time of strict controls on maritime traffic in the area. The tanker is said to have eventually followed instructions and no serious damage or injuries were reported from the encounter.

The incident comes amid persistent friction in the region with the United States having struck Iranian military positions near Qeshm Island and other spots along the strait while Iran works to manage or restrict passage through this narrow but essential corridor for world oil supplies.

Any prolonged interference with traffic here tends to lift gas prices at the pumps and complicate supply lines for economies that depend on stable global markets including those in North America.

On the social platform X opinions divide sharply as some right-leaning voices push for decisive steps to keep sea lanes open and deter challenges to international shipping norms whereas left-leaning commentators generally favour quicker talks and restraint to head off a wider confrontation.

🚨🇨🇺 BREAKING: Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel unveiled a sweeping set of economic reforms Friday, casting them as Cuba’s path through the crisis driven by a “criminal energy blockade” by the United States that has left the island nearly without fuel.

Only 1 oil tanker has reached Cuba in the past 5 months, Díaz-Canel said, a collapse that traces to January, when the U.S. captured Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro and cut the flow of Venezuelan oil, then threatened tariffs on any country that supplied Cuba. 

Havana now endures blackouts of up to 15 hours a day. Among the most devastating consequences of the U.S. policy is that the Cuban government has been forced to postpone tens of thousands of surgeries for its citizens. 

The Cuban President today named national defense as the first priority, citing preparations under Cuba’s “Whole People’s War” doctrine. The full 2026 reform program includes:

🔹Enterprise & Markets
🔸 Overhaul the economic management system, easing the conflict between central planning and incentives for production
🔸 Grant state enterprises autonomy to set their own wages, dimensions, clients, and suppliers, use their profits freely, and enter the currency market directly
🔸 Loosen rules on non-state businesses, narrowing banned activities and widening shareholding
🔸 Fast-track approval of stalled private and state MSMEs (mipymes)

🔹 Decentralization & the State
🔸 Expand municipal autonomy, letting provinces import, export, attract foreign investment, and manage hard-currency income on their own
🔸 Restructure and shrink the state, cutting ministries and posts via a public draft law to reduce bureaucracy

🔹 Trade & Investment
🔸 Make foreign trade more dynamic, dropping mandatory intermediaries and favoring importers of raw materials over finished goods 
🔸 Incentivize foreign direct investment, plus a new role for Cuban-diaspora and resident-Cuban investors as economic actors

🔹 Agriculture & Food
🔸 Reform land use, giving land to those who will farm it, cutting idle plots, and opening input and currency markets to producers across all sectors
🔸 Pursue food self-sufficiency by streamlining bureaucracy for agricultural production

🔹 Fiscal, Monetary & Social
🔸 Shift fiscal policy so the budget no longer finances state-enterprise inefficiency
🔸 Redesign monetary policy and the foreign-exchange market, and strengthen the banking system
🔸 Reform wages in the budget sector and move from subsidizing products to subsidizing people

🔹 Energy & Transport
🔸 Expand renewable energy and electric mobility, including local EV assembly, to cut dependence on imported fuel
🔸 Ease vehicle import limits, favoring solar-charged electric vehicles

🔹 Tourism, Commerce & Labor
🔸 Revive tourism through new operators beyond the big foreign chains that have pulled out
🔸 Modernize domestic commerce with electronic invoicing and digital platforms
🔸 Protect young skilled workers with better pay and incentives to curb emigration
